title: Infield Fly with Runner Break
description: You're the baserunner on 2nd base. Less than 2 outs, runner also on 1st. The batter pops up a high fly ball to shallow left field. The umpire signals infield fly. The left fielder is drifting under it but looks uncertain. What do you do?
options: Sprint to 3rd immediately — the fielder might drop it
Tag up at 2nd and wait for the catch, then advance
Stay on the base — you're safe no matter what since batter is out
Tag up at 2nd, read the fielder — advance only if the ball drops or he bobbles it
explanations: Dangerous! On an infield fly, the batter is out but the ball is LIVE. If you leave the base and the ball is caught, you can be doubled off. Sprinting without tagging is reckless.
Tagging up is smart thinking, but waiting for the catch before reading the situation is too conservative. If the ball drops, you've wasted time standing on the base.
Wrong! The batter is out on the infield fly, but you are NOT automatically safe. If the ball is caught and you're off the base, you can be thrown out. The ball is always live.

Perfect! On an infield fly, the batter is automatically out, but the ball is LIVE. Tag up to protect yourself, then read the fielder. If he drops it, you advance safely. If he catches it, you're still safe at 2nd. Best of both worlds.
concept: On an infield fly, the batter is out but the ball is live — tag up and read the play

title: Balk or Pick-off?
description: You're a left-handed pitcher on the mound. Runner on 1st is taking a big lead. You want to pick him off. The count is 2-1. You start your motion, lift your leg, then throw to first. The umpire calls a balk. Why?
options: Step toward 1st base and throw — you didn't fully commit to the plate
Step directly toward home but throw to 1st — your momentum was toward home
Pause in the middle of your delivery, then throw to 1st
Step toward 1st with a quick move but fail to disengage the rubber first
explanations: This is actually a legal pick-off move for a lefty! If you step toward 1st and throw, that's not a balk. The key is your step direction.

That's the balk! Once your body momentum commits toward home plate, you MUST deliver to the batter. Throwing to a base after committing home is a balk every time. A lefty's advantage is the step direction — step toward 1st and it's legal, step toward home and you must go home.
Pausing mid-delivery is also a balk, but that wasn't what happened here. The issue is your momentum direction, not a pause.

Disengaging the rubber first would actually make this legal — once you step off the rubber, you're no longer in the pitching position and can throw anywhere. The problem here is that you committed toward home.
concept: A pitcher's momentum direction determines balk — commit home, you must go home

title: Obstruction at Third
description: You're the baserunner rounding 2nd and heading for 3rd on a double to the gap. The third baseman, who doesn't have the ball yet, is standing in the baseline blocking your path. You have to slow down and go around him. What should you do?
options: Plow through him — you have the right of way
Stop and go back to 2nd to avoid a collision
Run around him and continue to 3rd — it's your problem to avoid the fielder
Run to 3rd, and if you're tagged out, signal to the umpire that obstruction occurred
explanations: Never intentionally plow into a fielder! That could get YOU called out for interference, plus it's unsportsmanlike and dangerous. The umpire should call obstruction, not you enforcing it.

Going back to 2nd gives up your rightful base. If the fielder blocks you without the ball, that's obstruction — the umpire should award you 3rd base. Don't penalize yourself for the fielder's mistake.
Running around him puts you at a disadvantage and costs you time. But more importantly, you shouldn't have to detour — a fielder without the ball cannot block the basepath. That's obstruction.

Correct! Continue your run to 3rd. If tagged out, the umpire should recognize the obstruction and award you the base you would have reached. Always keep running — let the umpire make the call. Stopping or going back only hurts you.
concept: Obstruction: a fielder without the ball cannot block the basepath — keep running and let the umpire call it

title: Appeal Play at Third
description: You're the manager. Runner on 3rd tags up on a deep fly ball to center and scores what appears to be the go-ahead run. But your third baseman tells you the runner left 3rd a split second before the catch. The next batter is already in the box. What do you do?
options: Tell the umpire the runner left early — they'll reverse the call
Have your pitcher step on 3rd base before the next pitch to appeal
Have your pitcher throw to 3rd base after properly disengaging the rubber
Nothing — once the runner touched home, the run counts no matter what
explanations: You can't just tell the umpire — an appeal play requires a physical action by the defense, not a verbal complaint. The umpire won't reverse the call on their own.
Close, but the pitcher can't just step on the base. The pitcher must properly appeal by throwing the ball to the base while the ball is live. Simply stepping on it doesn't constitute a proper appeal.

Correct! The pitcher must disengage the rubber, then throw to 3rd base for the appeal. If the umpire agrees the runner left early, the run comes off the board. This must happen before the next pitch is thrown or it's too late. Knowing appeal play mechanics can save games.
Wrong! A run can be nullified on a proper appeal even after the runner touches home. Appeal plays are one of the most overlooked weapons in baseball. You must appeal before the next pitch.
concept: Appeal plays require a throw to the base before the next pitch — verbal complaints don't count

title: Dropped Third Strike Dash
description: You're the batter. Two outs, nobody on base. The pitcher throws a nasty curveball and you swing and miss for strike three. But the ball bounces in the dirt and rolls away from the catcher! What do you do?
options: Walk back to the dugout — you struck out
Run to first base as fast as you can!
Stay in the box and wait for the umpire to tell you what to do
Argue with the umpire that the pitch was in the dirt
explanations: Don't give up! When the catcher drops strike three with two outs and first base is open, you can run to first. Walking away means you missed a free chance to get on base.

Yes! Sprint to first! On a dropped third strike, if first base is unoccupied (or there are two outs), you can run to first base. The catcher has to throw you out or tag you. This is one of the most exciting plays in baseball — never give up on a dropped third strike!
Don't wait — GO! Every second counts. The catcher is scrambling to pick up the ball. The faster you run, the better your chance of being safe.
Arguing wastes time when you should be RUNNING! The pitch being in the dirt is actually great for you — it means the catcher probably can't throw you out. Run!
concept: On a dropped third strike with first base open (or 2 outs), you can run to first — never give up!

title: Double Cut Relay from Deep Center
description: You're the shortstop. A ball is hit to the deepest part of center field, 380+ feet. Runner on 1st is flying around the bases. The center fielder has the ball and is setting up to throw. Where do you position yourself for the relay?
options: Go out to shallow center for a single cutoff throw to home
Line up between the center fielder and 3rd base for a relay to third
Line up in a tandem relay with the second baseman — you're the lead man in line with home plate
Stay at shortstop position and let the outfielder throw all the way home
explanations: A single cutoff from shallow center is too far — the throw from 380+ feet needs two relay men to maintain accuracy. One cutoff on a ball this deep results in a weak, offline throw.
Throwing to 3rd doesn't help if the runner is already rounding third and heading home. You need to set up the relay to home plate, not to a base the runner has already passed.

Perfect! On balls hit to the deepest gaps, the shortstop and second baseman form a tandem (double) relay. You're the lead man lined up between the outfielder and home plate. The second baseman backs you up 15-20 feet behind. This two-man relay keeps throws short and accurate. The outfielder throws to you, you spin and fire to the trail man or directly home.
Never let the outfielder try to throw 380 feet to home! That's a guaranteed overthrow or a weak bounce. Relay throws exist to keep the ball moving fast and accurate through the cutoff men.
concept: On deep gap hits, shortstop and second baseman form a tandem relay lined up with home plate

title: Backup Responsibilities on Overthrow
description: You're the right fielder. The pitcher just fielded a ground ball and threw to first base, but the throw sails over the first baseman's head toward the right field foul area. Runner is on 2nd base. What should you have been doing?
options: Watching from your position — the first baseman should handle it
Already sprinting toward the foul area behind first base to back up the throw
Running toward 2nd base to cover in case the runner tries to advance
Calling out to the first baseman to catch the ball
explanations: Watching from your position is a mental error. Right fielders ALWAYS back up throws to first base. This is one of your most important defensive responsibilities, and you should be moving before the throw is even released.

Exactly right! On ANY throw to first base, the right fielder sprints to back up the throw. You should start moving the moment the ball is fielded — not when the throw is made. If the throw gets past the first baseman (like this one), you're there to field it and prevent the runner on 2nd from scoring. Backing up is one of the most underrated skills in outfield defense.

Your job is to back up first base, not cover 2nd. The shortstop or second baseman handles coverage at 2nd. Each position has specific backup assignments, and right fielders back up first base on all infield throws.
Yelling doesn't help. Your legs and positioning are what prevent extra bases. The right fielder's backup responsibility on throws to first is one of the first things you should learn in the outfield.
concept: Right fielders ALWAYS back up throws to first base — start moving before the throw

title: Pop Fly Priority Collision
description: You're the catcher. A high pop fly is hit between home plate and the pitcher's mound. You, the pitcher, and the third baseman are all converging on the ball. Who has priority?
options: The pitcher — he's closest to where it was hit
You (the catcher) — you're facing the ball and have the best angle
The third baseman — infielders always have priority over the battery
Call off both of them and take it yourself since you can see the whole field
explanations: The pitcher should NEVER take pop flies near the plate. Pitchers field pop-ups poorly because they're spinning around disoriented. The pitcher's job is to get out of the way when someone else calls for it.

Correct! The catcher has priority on pop flies near home plate. You're the only player facing the right direction (toward the field), giving you the best read on the ball. Call 'I GOT IT!' loud and early. The pitcher and third baseman should back off when they hear you. The catcher is like a quarterback on pop-ups — you direct traffic.
Infielders have priority over outfielders, but the CATCHER has priority over everyone on pops near the plate. The third baseman yields to you in this area.

Calling them off and taking it yourself IS the right idea — that's exactly what 'you have priority' means. But the answer is specifically about understanding the priority system, not just the action. You call for it because the rules of priority give you that right.
concept: The catcher has priority on pop flies near home plate — call for it loud and early

title: Squeeze Play Decision
description: You're the manager. Bottom of the 8th, tied game. Runner on 3rd with 1 out. Your #7 hitter is at the plate — he's a good bunter but weak hitter (.210 average). The opposing pitcher is rattled and has walked the last two batters. What's your call?
options: Call a suicide squeeze — bunt the runner home
Let him swing away — the pitcher is wild, maybe he walks too
Call a safety squeeze — bunt only if the pitch is a strike
Put on the hit-and-run to move the runner
explanations: A suicide squeeze is aggressive but risky here. If the pitcher throws a pitchout or the batter misses the bunt, the runner is dead at home. With a wild pitcher who might walk your hitter anyway, the risk isn't worth it.

Swinging away with a .210 hitter is unlikely to produce a hit. While a walk is possible, you're leaving the outcome to chance. With a runner 90 feet from scoring the go-ahead run, you should manufacture the run.

Perfect! The safety squeeze gives you the best of both worlds. The runner doesn't break until the ball is bunted, eliminating the risk of a pitchout or missed bunt killing you. If the batter gets a good pitch to bunt, he drops it down and the runner scores. If the pitch is bad, the batter takes it — maybe draws a walk. A .210 hitter who's a good bunter is PERFECT for the safety squeeze.
Hit-and-run with a runner on 3rd is almost never done. The runner on 3rd can't 'run' — he's already at 3rd. The hit-and-run is for runners on 1st or 2nd who need to advance.
concept: The safety squeeze is lower risk than the suicide squeeze — runner only goes when the ball is bunted

title: Run-Down Technique
description: You're the second baseman. A runner is caught in a rundown between 1st and 2nd. The first baseman has the ball and is chasing the runner toward you at 2nd base. What's the proper technique?
options: Stay on the 2nd base bag and wait for the throw
Run toward the runner to close the gap, then tag him
Position yourself on the 1st base side of 2nd, give a target, and receive the throw when the runner commits toward 2nd
Chase the runner back toward 1st base so the first baseman can tag him
explanations: Standing on the bag limits your ability to react. In a rundown, you need to be ready to receive the throw and tag the runner, not anchor yourself to the base.
Running toward the runner creates a potential collision and makes it harder to receive the throw cleanly. You need to let the first baseman drive the runner toward you, not chase him yourself.

Exactly! Proper rundown technique: position yourself on the inside (1st base side) of 2nd, give a clear target, and receive the throw when the runner commits toward you. The goal is to get the out with ONE throw — the first baseman drives the runner toward you, you receive and tag. Multiple throws increase error chance. Keep the ball on the infield-side to avoid hitting the runner in the back.

Chasing the runner back creates chaos and multiple throws, which increases error probability. The rule is: the fielder with the ball drives the runner toward the OTHER fielder. You receive, you don't chase.
concept: In a rundown, the receiving fielder positions on the inside and takes the ball with one throw

title: Shift Defense Against Pull Hitter
description: You're the third baseman. The batter is a dead-pull left-handed hitter who hits 80% of ground balls to the right side. There's a runner on 1st with 1 out in a 1-run game. Your coach signals the shift. Where should you position?
options: Stay at normal third base depth and position — don't leave the line open
Move to the shortstop position, leaving 3rd base open
Move a few steps toward shortstop but stay close enough to cover 3rd
Charge toward the plate expecting a bunt since 3rd is undefended
explanations: Staying in normal position ignores the scouting data. If 80% of his ground balls go to the right side, you're standing where only 20% of the action is. That's bad positioning.

Moving all the way to shortstop is too extreme. While the stats say he pulls, you're in a 1-run game with a runner on. Leaving the entire left side of the infield undefended allows an easy bunt single or a rare opposite-field hit to be a guaranteed double.

Smart! You shift a few steps toward short but keep 3rd base in range. This balances the scouting data (80% pull) with game context (1-run game, runner on). You take away some of the pull-side ground balls while still being able to recover if he goes the other way. In a close game, extreme shifts are risky because one hit to the open side can change everything.

There's no reason to expect a bunt here. The batter is a pull hitter, not a bunter. Charging the plate leaves a massive hole in the infield and takes you out of the play on his most likely batted ball.
concept: In close games, balance the shift with game context — don't leave positions completely undefended

title: First and Third Defense
description: You're the catcher. Runners on 1st and 3rd, 1 out. The runner on 1st breaks for 2nd on the pitch. You know the runner on 3rd is fast and will try to score if you throw to 2nd. What's your play?
options: Throw to 2nd base — get the steal out
Pump-fake to 2nd, then throw back to 3rd to catch the runner
Stand up and hold the ball — don't throw anywhere
Throw to the pitcher who cuts it off and checks the runner at 3rd
explanations: Throwing straight to 2nd gives the runner on 3rd a clear lane to score. By the time the ball reaches 2nd, the runner from 3rd is halfway home. You're trading a base for a run — terrible trade in a 1-run game.
The pump-fake to 2nd can work, but experienced runners on 3rd won't commit on a fake throw. They watch your feet, not your arm. And now the runner from 1st is safe at 2nd anyway.
Holding the ball concedes 2nd base for free. While it prevents the run, it also puts the tying run in scoring position with no benefit. There's a better option that addresses both runners.

Perfect! The pitcher cut play is the smartest option. You throw to the pitcher (or shortstop cutting in front of 2nd), who looks the runner back to 3rd. If the runner on 3rd broke too far, the cut man throws him out. If not, you've at least kept the runner at 3rd while the runner from 1st only reaches 2nd. This is a team play that addresses both threats.
concept: On first-and-third steals, use the cut play through the pitcher or middle infielder

title: Pitch Count Awareness
description: You're a 10-year-old pitcher in the 4th inning. You've already thrown 55 pitches. Your arm feels a little tired. There's a runner on 2nd with 1 out. Your coach hasn't said anything about taking you out. What should you do?
options: Keep throwing as hard as you can — your team needs you!
Tell your coach your arm feels tired
Try to finish the inning, then tell your coach
Ignore the tired feeling — all pitchers get tired
explanations: Throwing hard when your arm is tired is how injuries happen! Young arms are still growing, and pushing through fatigue can cause serious damage that affects you for years. Never throw through pain or fatigue.

This is the right answer EVERY TIME. Telling your coach your arm is tired is brave, not weak. Your arm is still growing, and protecting it is the most important thing. Good coaches want to know when you're tired. Your health matters more than any game.
Trying to finish the inning might only be 2-3 more batters, but each pitch with a tired arm increases injury risk. Don't gamble with your arm health. Tell your coach NOW.

Every pitcher gets tired, but ignoring it is dangerous for young arms. There are rules about pitch counts for a reason — your body is still developing. A tired arm that keeps throwing can lead to injuries that last a lifetime.
concept: Young pitchers MUST tell their coach when their arm is tired — protecting your arm is more important than any game

title: Tag Play at Home
description: You're the catcher. A runner on 3rd is trying to score on a ground ball to the shortstop. The shortstop throws home to you. The ball arrives just before the runner. How do you make the tag?
options: Block the plate with your body and wait for the ball
Catch the ball and swipe down with your glove in front of the plate
Stand behind the plate and reach out to tag the runner
Catch the ball first, then give the runner a lane to the plate and tag him as he slides
explanations: Blocking the plate without the ball is obstruction! Since the new rules, you must give the runner a lane to the plate. Blocking can result in the runner being called safe AND you getting hurt in a collision.
Swiping blindly can cause you to miss the tag or drop the ball. You need to secure the catch first, then make a controlled tag. A sweep tag is effective but only after you've caught the ball cleanly.
Standing behind the plate puts you too far from the action. You need to be positioned where you can receive the throw AND apply the tag efficiently.

Correct! Under current rules, you must give the runner a lane to the plate. Catch the ball FIRST (secure it!), then apply the tag as the runner slides in. Position yourself on the 3rd base side of the plate, receive the throw, and sweep the tag low. Catch first, tag second — in that order.
concept: At home plate: catch first, give a lane, then tag — blocking without the ball is obstruction

title: Two-Strike Approach
description: You're at the plate with 2 strikes. You've been swinging for the fences all game but struck out twice. The pitcher is throwing hard. What should you change?
options: Keep swinging hard — one big hit can change the game
Choke up on the bat, shorten your swing, and try to make contact
Move closer to the plate to cover the outside corner
Close your eyes and swing as hard as you can
explanations: Swinging for the fences with 2 strikes is why you struck out twice already. With 2 strikes, a strikeout is the WORST outcome. You need to change your approach, not repeat the same mistake.

Yes! The two-strike approach is one of the most important things a batter can learn. Choke up 1-2 inches, shorten your swing, and focus on putting the ball in play. A single, a walk, or even a foul ball to stay alive is better than another strikeout. Many of baseball's best hitters change their swing with 2 strikes.
Moving closer to the plate doesn't help and might get you hit by an inside pitch. The adjustment should be in your SWING, not your position in the box.
This is silly and dangerous! Never close your eyes when a ball is coming at you. You need to see the ball to hit the ball. Watch it all the way into the bat.
concept: With 2 strikes, choke up and shorten your swing — making contact beats striking out every time

title: Hit-and-Run Execution
description: You're the batter. Your coach signals hit-and-run. The runner on 1st will break for 2nd on the pitch. The count is 1-1. What is your MAIN job?
options: Swing as hard as you can and try to hit a home run
Swing at the pitch no matter what — you MUST make contact
Take the pitch to see if the runner gets a good jump
Try to hit the ball to the right side to protect the runner
explanations: The hit-and-run isn't about power — it's about PROTECTING the runner. Swinging for the fences often means a swing and miss, leaving the runner hung out to dry on a stolen base attempt.

You must swing on a hit-and-run, but just making contact isn't enough. The DIRECTION matters. If you hit a line drive to the shortstop, the runner is doubled off 1st. You need to hit it to the right side where the second baseman has vacated to cover the steal.
Never take the pitch on a hit-and-run! The runner is GOING — if you don't swing, it becomes a straight steal, and the catcher has a clean throw to 2nd. You must swing to protect the runner.

Exactly! On a hit-and-run, your main job is to hit the ball on the ground to the right side. Why? The second baseman or shortstop has moved to cover 2nd for the steal, leaving a hole on the right side. A ground ball there advances the runner safely and often produces a hit. This is smart, team baseball — not about your stats, about moving the runner.
concept: On a hit-and-run, hit the ball to the right side to protect the runner and exploit the defensive hole

title: Ground Ball to Short with Runner on Third
description: You're the shortstop. Runner on 3rd base, 1 out. A ground ball is hit right at you. The runner on 3rd starts sprinting home. What do you do with the ball?
options: Throw home to get the runner — stop the run!
Throw to first base to get the sure out
Look the runner back to 3rd, then throw to first
Hold the ball and run at the baserunner to start a rundown
explanations: Throwing home is risky! If the throw is late or offline, the runner scores AND the batter reaches first safely. With 1 out, you can get the batter at first and still have a chance to get the next out.
Getting the sure out at first is safe, but you're just conceding the run. If you can at least LOOK the runner back, you might keep him at 3rd AND get the out at first.

Perfect! This is heads-up baseball. When you look the runner back to 3rd, he has to hesitate or go back. Then you throw to first for the sure out. Now there are 2 outs and the runner is still on 3rd. You've kept the game tied AND gotten an out. This is the smart play that separates good infielders from great ones.
Running at the baserunner takes you out of position and wastes time. The batter is running to first — every second counts. Make the play efficiently with a look and a throw.
concept: With a runner on 3rd, look the runner back THEN throw to first for the sure out

title: Sacrifice Fly Depth
description: You're the left fielder. Runner on 3rd, 1 out, tie game. The batter hits a medium-deep fly ball to you — it's catchable but not a routine catch. You're playing at normal depth. As you catch it, you realize the runner will easily tag and score. What should you have done differently?
options: Played deeper to have more time to throw after the catch
Played shallow to prevent the runner from tagging up
Let the ball drop for a single to keep the runner at 3rd
Played at normal depth — there's nothing you could have done differently
explanations: Playing deeper gives you a longer throw to home AND the runner more time to tag. That's the opposite of what you want. Deeper positioning only helps if you're worried about the ball going over your head.

Correct! With a runner on 3rd and less than 2 outs, you should play SHALLOW. Why? A sacrifice fly scores the run anyway, so your job is to take away the sac fly. If you play shallow, medium-depth fly balls become outs that the runner CAN'T tag on because the throw home is short and strong. Yes, a ball might go over your head, but preventing the sure run is worth the risk of an extra-base hit.
Letting the ball drop intentionally is terrible. A single scores the runner from 3rd just as easily as a sac fly, and now the batter is on base too. You've given up the run AND put another runner on.
There IS something you could have done — play shallow! Knowing the game situation should change your positioning. Great outfielders adjust their depth based on the situation, not just the hitter.
concept: With a runner on 3rd and less than 2 outs, play shallow to take away the sacrifice fly

title: Intentional Walk Setup
description: You're the manager. Bottom 9th, up by 1, runner on 2nd, 1 out. Their best hitter (.340 avg, 30 HR) is at the plate. The next batter is hitting .220. First base is open. What's your call?
options: Pitch to the .340 hitter — don't put the winning run on base
Intentionally walk him to set up a force at every base and face the .220 hitter
Walk him and bring in a relief pitcher to face the .220 hitter
Pitch around him carefully — don't walk him but don't give him anything good
explanations: Pitching to their best hitter with the game on the line is brave but foolish. A .340 hitter with 30 homers can end the game with one swing. You're risking a walk-off hit against the most dangerous bat in the lineup when a much easier option exists.

Exactly right! Walking the .340 hitter to face the .220 hitter is textbook managing. You now have: force play at every base (including home), the double play is in order, and you're facing a MUCH weaker hitter. The 'winning run on base' doesn't matter because a hit from the .340 guy scores the run from 2nd anyway. You're not giving up anything — you're taking away their best weapon and creating defensive options.

Walking and changing pitchers might be overthinking it. If your current pitcher has been effective, keep him in against the weaker hitter. A pitching change could backfire — the new pitcher is cold and the .220 hitter might get a lucky hit. Only change if the matchup strongly favors it.
Pitching around him risks a 2-strike count where you're forced to throw a strike. A .340 hitter with power can punish any pitch in the zone. An intentional walk is cleaner and removes all ambiguity.
concept: Walk the dangerous hitter to set up force plays and face the weaker batter behind him

title: Covering First on a Bunt
description: You're the first baseman. The other team has a fast runner on 1st, and you notice the batter squaring around to bunt. Your coach has told you the bunt defense. What's your move?
options: Stay at first base to keep the runner close
Charge hard toward the plate to field the bunt
Charge toward the plate, field the bunt, and throw to 2nd for the force
Wait to see where the bunt goes before moving
explanations: Staying at first base means nobody is there to field a bunt toward the first base side. The batter will bunt for a hit and everyone is safe. You have to charge.

Right! On a bunt play, the first baseman charges hard toward the plate. Your job is to field any bunt to the right side and make the play. The pitcher covers the middle, and the third baseman covers the left side. The second baseman rotates to cover first base for you. This is a team play — everyone has a job.

Charging and throwing to 2nd sounds great, but it's very difficult. By the time you field the bunt and turn to throw to 2nd, the runner is usually already there. The safe play is to field the bunt and throw to first (where the second baseman is covering) to get the sure out on the batter.

Waiting to see where the bunt goes wastes precious time. Bunts are FAST plays — the batter is sprinting to first the moment the bat touches the ball. You need to be moving BEFORE the bunt to have any chance of making the play.
concept: On a bunt, the first baseman charges hard — the second baseman covers first base behind you

title: Reading the Pitcher's Pickoff Move
description: You're on 1st base. The left-handed pitcher keeps looking at you. You want to steal 2nd but you need to read his move. What's the key tell that tells you he's going HOME instead of throwing to first?
options: Watch his glove — if it goes up, he's throwing to first
Watch his right foot — if it steps toward home, he's delivering to the plate
Watch his left heel — once it lifts past his knee, he MUST go home
Watch his eyes — if he looks at the catcher, he's going home
explanations: The glove isn't a reliable tell. Pitchers can move their glove the same way on both pickoff attempts and deliveries. You need a more definitive read.
The right foot (stride foot) stepping toward home is a tell, but by the time you see this, the pitcher is already deep in his motion. You need an EARLIER read to get a good jump on the steal.

This is the key! For a left-handed pitcher, watch the left heel. Once the left heel lifts past the knee, the pitcher MUST deliver to home plate — it's a balk if he throws to first after that. This is the earliest and most reliable read against a lefty. The moment you see that heel rise past the knee, GO! This is the splitest of split seconds that separates great base stealers from average ones.
Eyes are unreliable. Good pitchers deliberately look at the catcher while throwing over to first, or look at the runner while delivering home. Experienced pitchers use their eyes to deceive you.
concept: Against a lefty, read the left heel — once it lifts past the knee, the pitcher must go home

title: Full Count with Bases Loaded Walk
description: You're the batter. The count is 3-2 (full count). Bases are loaded with 2 outs. Your team is down by 1 run. The pitcher is nervous and has been missing his spots. What's your approach?
options: Swing at everything — you can't let a strike go by!
Be patient — only swing at a pitch you can drive. A walk ties the game!
Bunt to surprise the defense
Swing for a home run — grand slam wins it!
explanations: Swinging at everything means you'll chase bad pitches. The pitcher is already struggling with control — if you're patient, he might walk you and tie the game. Swinging at a ball in the dirt helps HIM, not you.

Perfect! This is one of the hardest things in baseball — being patient with 2 strikes. But think about it: a walk TIES THE GAME. The pitcher is nervous and missing spots. If you only swing at pitches in your zone, one of two things happens: you get a hit (great!) or you get walked (ties the game!). Discipline at the plate wins more games than big swings.
Never bunt with bases loaded and 2 outs! A bunt is almost always an easy out, and you'd be giving up the chance to tie the game with a walk or win it with a hit.
Swinging for the fences sounds exciting but it leads to strikeouts. With bases loaded, a single ties the game just as well. Trying to do too much is how you strike out in big moments.
concept: With bases loaded and a full count, patience is power — a walk is as good as a hit

title: Relay Throw Alignment
description: You're the second baseman. A ball is hit to deep right-center gap. The runner on 1st is heading for 3rd. The center fielder picks up the ball. Where do you position yourself for the relay?
options: Run out toward the outfield and line up between the center fielder and 3rd base
Run out toward the outfield and line up between the center fielder and home plate
Stay at your position near 2nd base and let the shortstop handle the relay
Go cover 2nd base in case the batter tries to advance
explanations: Correct! On a ball hit to right-center with the throw going to 3rd base, the second baseman goes out for the relay and lines up between the outfielder and 3rd base. Your arms go up so the outfielder can see your target. You catch the relay throw and redirect it to 3rd. The shortstop covers 2nd base behind you. Every relay play has a specific alignment — yours is outfielder-to-you-to-third.
Lining up with home plate is what the SHORTSTOP does when the play is at home. On this play, the runner is going to 3rd, not home. Your relay alignment should point at 3rd base, not home plate.

Staying at your position is a mental error. On extra-base hits to the outfield, middle infielders MUST go out for relay duty. You can't expect the outfielder to make a 300-foot throw accurately. The relay system exists to keep throws short and on target.
Covering 2nd is the shortstop's job on this play. When the second baseman goes out for the relay, the shortstop rotates to cover 2nd. Every player has an assignment — know yours.
concept: On balls hit to right-center with a play at 3rd, the second baseman aligns the relay between the outfielder and 3rd base

title: Communication on Fly Ball in the Gap
description: You're the center fielder. A fly ball is hit to the gap between you and the right fielder. You can both reach it. You're running toward it and so is the right fielder. What do you do?
options: Let the right fielder take it — he's closer
Both try to catch it — somebody will get it
Call 'I GOT IT!' loud and early, then wave off the right fielder
Stay quiet and run harder — the first person there gets it
explanations: You might think the right fielder is closer, but as the center fielder, YOU have priority on all balls in the gap. The center fielder is the captain of the outfield. Don't defer when it's your ball.
Both going for it without communication is how collisions happen. Two players running full speed into each other can cause serious injuries. SOMEONE must call for the ball.

Exactly! The center fielder has priority over the corner outfielders on ANY ball you can both reach. Call 'I GOT IT!' loud enough for the right fielder to hear, wave your arms, and take charge. The right fielder should peel off and back you up. This communication prevents collisions and dropped balls. The center fielder is the quarterback of the outfield.

Staying quiet is dangerous! Without communication, both players converge and either collide or both pull off at the last second and the ball drops. Call for the ball EARLY and LOUD. Communication saves games and prevents injuries.
concept: The center fielder has priority on gap fly balls — call early and loud, wave off the corner outfielder

title: Pickoff Move from First
description: You're the first baseman. There's a fast runner on 1st who's taking an aggressive lead. The pitcher has been ignoring him. You signal the pitcher for a pickoff play. The pitcher throws over. How do you make the tag?
options: Stand behind the runner so you can tag him when he dives back
Position yourself behind the base (on the outfield side) and sweep the tag down in front of the base
Straddle the base so the runner can't get back
Position yourself between the runner and the base with your glove low, ready to receive and sweep the tag
explanations: Standing behind the runner puts you too far from the base. The tag needs to happen at the base, not behind the runner. You'll never reach him in time.
Being on the outfield side of the base puts you out of position. The runner is diving back toward the INFIELD side of the base. You need to be where the runner is going to be, not on the other side.
Straddling the base blocks the runner's path, which could be called obstruction. You can't block the base without having the ball. Even with the ball, straddling makes it harder to apply a clean tag.

Perfect! Position yourself between the runner and the base, on the infield side. Keep your glove low and open, ready to receive the throw. When the ball arrives, catch it and sweep the tag down in front of the base — right where the runner's hand will be diving back. The key is glove position: low, in front of the base, on the runner's side. This gives you the quickest tag possible.
concept: On pickoff tags at first, position between the runner and the base with a low sweep tag

title: Suicide Squeeze Recognition
description: You're the pitcher. Runner on 3rd, 1 out, 1-run game. As you start your windup, you notice the runner on 3rd breaking for home AND the batter squaring to bunt. You recognize it's a suicide squeeze. What do you do?
options: Throw your best fastball for a strike — challenge the bunter
Throw a pitchout — high and inside, away from the batter
Step off the rubber and throw to 3rd to catch the runner
Throw the pitch way inside — make the bunt impossible
explanations: Throwing a strike on a suicide squeeze is exactly what the offense wants. The bunter just needs to get the bat on the ball — any bunt in play scores the run. Don't give them a pitch they can bunt.

A pitchout is a good idea in concept, but a TRUE pitchout (catcher stands up and moves outside) is obvious and might not work if the batter can still reach it. Throwing inside is more effective because it jams the batter.
You can't step off and throw to 3rd — by the time you recognize the squeeze and disengage the rubber, the runner is already too close to home. This play happens too fast for a step-off throw.

Correct! The best defense against a suicide squeeze is to throw the ball way inside (or even at the batter's hands). This makes the bunt nearly impossible — the batter either can't reach it, or fouls it off, or gets jammed and pops it up. The runner from 3rd is committed and will be hung out to dry if the bunt fails. This is the one time it's okay to throw inside intentionally on a bunt situation.
concept: Against a suicide squeeze, throw inside to make the bunt impossible — the runner is committed and stranded

title: Two-Out Rally Baserunning
description: You're on 2nd base with 2 outs. Your teammate hits a ground ball to the shortstop. The ball is hit sharply. What do you do?
options: Stay at 2nd and wait to see if the shortstop makes an error
Run to 3rd base and stop there
Sprint full speed toward home — run on ANYTHING with 2 outs
Jog toward 3rd and see what happens
explanations: Waiting at 2nd base with 2 outs wastes a chance to score. If the shortstop bobbles it or makes a bad throw, you should already be running. You can't score from 2nd if you're standing still.
Stopping at 3rd makes no sense with 2 outs. The inning ends if the batter is thrown out at first. Going to 3rd is pointless because you need to score to matter. It's all or nothing with 2 outs.

YES! With 2 outs, you run on ANYTHING hit on the ground. Here's why: the play is almost always going to first base. If the defense gets the out at first, the inning is over and it doesn't matter where you are. But if they bobble it, make a bad throw, or the batter beats it out — you've already scored because you were running full speed! This is the #1 rule of 2-out baserunning.

Jogging is never acceptable in baseball, especially with 2 outs. Every play could be the last play of the inning. Sprint full speed and let the play develop. Jogging means you'll be stranded at 3rd when the inning ends.
concept: With 2 outs, run full speed on any ground ball — the play goes to first, so you run home

title: Lefty vs Righty Matchup Decision
description: You're the manager in a tie game, bottom 8th. Your team has runners on 1st and 2nd with 1 out. The opposing manager brings in a tough left-handed reliever. Your current batter is a left-handed hitter (.195 avg vs LHP). You have a right-handed pinch hitter on the bench (.275 avg vs LHP). What do you do?
options: Keep the lefty in — he's been hitting well today
Pinch hit with the righty who hits .275 against lefties
Have the batter bunt the runners over to 2nd and 3rd
Call for a double steal to take pressure off the batter
explanations: Today's performance doesn't override a .195 average against lefties. Small sample size in one game vs. a season of data. A lefty hitting .195 against left-handed pitching is at a massive disadvantage — the platoon split is real and significant.

Correct! The platoon advantage is one of the most reliable edges in baseball. Right-handed hitters see the ball better against left-handed pitchers because the ball breaks TOWARD them instead of away. Your .275 righty has an 80-point advantage over your .195 lefty in this matchup. In a tie game with runners in scoring position, you play the percentages. This is exactly what the bench is for.

Bunting gives up an out to move runners from 1st-and-2nd to 2nd-and-3rd. With 1 out, that puts the tying run at 3rd — but you already have runners in scoring position. Plus, you'd now have 2 outs, meaning a strikeout ends the inning. A hit is more valuable than a bunt here.

A double steal is risky with runners on 1st and 2nd. If the catcher throws through and gets the runner at 3rd, you've killed the rally. Don't get aggressive with base running when the better move is at the plate.
concept: The platoon advantage is real — right-handed hitters perform significantly better against left-handed pitchers

title: Throwing to the Wrong Base
description: You're the left fielder. There's a runner on 1st and the batter hits a single to left field. The runner from 1st is rounding 2nd and heading for 3rd. The batter is rounding 1st. Where do you throw?
options: Throw to 3rd base to try to get the lead runner
Throw to 2nd base to keep the batter from advancing
Throw to the cutoff man (shortstop) who can redirect the throw
Hold the ball and run it in to the infield
explanations: Throwing to 3rd might seem right, but a throw from left field directly to 3rd often sails or bounces. If it gets past the third baseman, both runners advance an extra base. Long throws with no cutoff are risky.

Throwing to 2nd lets the lead runner reach 3rd easily and doesn't create any pressure on the defense. The batter is unlikely to try for 2nd on a single to left anyway. This throw accomplishes nothing.

Yes! Always throw to your cutoff man. The shortstop positions himself in the cutoff line between you and 3rd base. A strong throw to the cutoff man keeps BOTH runners honest. The cutoff man can redirect to 3rd if the lead runner rounds too far, throw to 2nd if the batter gets greedy, or eat the ball if everyone is safe. The relay system keeps everything under control.

Holding the ball and running it in is the WORST option. While you jog it in, the lead runner takes 3rd easily, and the batter might take 2nd. Always get the ball to an infielder quickly — every second matters.
concept: Always throw to the cutoff man — the relay system keeps all runners honest and prevents overthrows

title: 3-0 Count Green Light
description: You're the batter. Count is 3-0. You're one of the team's best hitters. Runner on 2nd, 1 out, your team is down by 2. Your coach has given you the green light to swing on 3-0. The pitcher throws a fastball right down the middle. What do you do?
options: Take the pitch — 3-0 is always a take. You'll be 3-1 and still ahead.
Swing aggressively — this is exactly the pitch you're looking for!
Swing but protect — don't overswing, just make contact
Check with the coach again before swinging
explanations: In the old days, 3-0 was always a take. But modern baseball has evolved. When your coach gives you the green light and you get a fastball down the middle, that might be the best pitch you see all at-bat. Taking it on principle wastes a golden opportunity. A 3-1 count is still good, but you'll likely get a tougher pitch next time.

Exactly! The 3-0 green light means your coach trusts you to recognize and crush a hittable pitch. A fastball down the middle on 3-0 is statistically the most hittable pitch in baseball — hitters bat over .400 on 3-0 fastballs in the zone. The pitcher is trying to get back in the count and throws a cookie. Attack it! You're down 2 with a runner on 2nd — this could change the game.

Just making contact wastes the best pitch advantage in baseball. On 3-0, the pitcher is behind and usually throws a fastball for a strike. This is YOUR pitch to drive. Don't minimize your swing when you have the count in your favor and the green light.
The coach already gave you the green light before the at-bat. Checking again wastes the moment. Trust the sign, trust your preparation, and attack the pitch.
concept: On 3-0 with the green light, attack fastballs in the zone — it's statistically the most hittable pitch in baseball
